     Edit the Claude source, not this file — regenerate instead. -->

> **Plane MCP pre-flight (Copilot — check this before the Plane path steps below):**
>
> Scan your available tool list for at least one tool matching `mcp__plane__*`, `plane_*`, or `mcp_plane_*`.
> - **None found → STOP.** Print: "Error: Plane MCP tools are not accessible. In VS Code, open the MCP panel (View → MCP Servers), ensure the `plane` server shows as Connected, and retry. Do not fall back to creating markdown tickets." Do not continue to the Markdown path.
> - **Found → note the active prefix** (e.g. `mcp__plane__` or `plane_`) and use it for every tool call in this session.
> - **If some `plane_*` tools are found but write tools like `create_work_item` are absent**, the tool list was truncated due to context budget overflow — re-run `install.sh` to enable the tool-filter proxy, then restart VS Code.
>
> **If `.claude/plane-config.md` exists, the Markdown path is off-limits** regardless of MCP status — never silently degrade to local TKT-*.md files when the project uses Plane.


# Start a New Plan

Given a brain dump (prose describing an initiative, expansion, or refactor), produce a **plan ticket** — a regular Plane work item carrying the `plan-ticket` label — whose description is the articulated guiding principles, with child work items capturing the specific work items needed to execute it.

This is the "plan / brainstorm" side of Plane. The plan ticket is the container of intent; the children are the execution. `/plan-verify` later judges the children's shipped work against the plan ticket's description.

## Pre-flight: detect backend

- If `.claude/plane-config.md` exists → **Plane path**.
- Else: error. `/plan-new` is Plane-only. Markdown-backed projects already have `/brainstorm` which covers the stub-generation case; no ports.

## Input

Either:
- `"<brain dump>"` — inline prose argument (any length; usually a paragraph to a page)
- `--from-file <path>` — read the brain dump from a file. Useful for long dumps or transcripts.

If neither is given, error with usage.

## Steps

1. **Read the brain dump** from argument or file.

2. **Read context** to inform the plan:
   - `.claude/plane-config.md` (project ID, state + label IDs)
   - `.claude/ticket-config.md` (key source locations, context docs)
   - `CLAUDE.md` (project rules)
   - The top couple of context docs listed in `ticket-config.md` (so the plan matches project conventions)

3. **Extract three things from the dump** (this is the core LLM work):

   **(a) Guiding principles** — invariants that every child ticket should respect. Things like "never break v1 API consumers", "UI touches must stay under 300 LOC per file", "all auth paths audit-logged with request ID". Concrete, observable. 2–6 principles is typical; more than 10 is a sign the plan is too broad.

   **(b) Candidate work items** — specific, bounded units of work that deliver on the principles. Each is a ~1-line title + ~1-paragraph description + draft acceptance criteria. Aim for 3–12 candidates; more than 15 means break this into two plans.

   **(c) Open questions** — things the dump didn't answer but need answering before work starts. Framed as direct questions Dan (or whoever) can resolve.

   **Separation discipline (load-bearing):** principles go in the plan-ticket's description; candidate work items become child tickets; open questions go as an initial comment on the plan ticket. Don't conflate — a principle is "what does success mean"; a ticket is "what specific work needs doing"; a question is "what don't we know yet."

4. **Create the plan ticket** via `mcp__plane__create_work_item`:
   - `project_id` from config
   - `name` = concise title derived from the brain dump (infer)
   - `state` = Backlog
   - `priority` = `medium` (let Dan adjust)
   - `description_html` = the principles, rendered as:
     ```html
     <h3>Guiding principles</h3>
     <ul>
       <li>{principle 1}</li>
       <li>{principle 2}</li>
       …
     </ul>
     <p><em>This plan ticket defines the intent. Child work items (see the children panel in Plane) execute it. <code>/plan-verify</code> judges shipped children against these principles.</em></p>
     ```
   - `labels` = `[plan-ticket]` — this is the signal that makes the work item a plan ticket.

5. **Post open questions as a comment** via `mcp__plane__create_work_item_comment`:
   ```html
   <h4>Open questions from the brain dump</h4>
   <ol>
     <li>{question 1}</li>
     <li>{question 2}</li>
     …
   </ol>
   ```
   Skip if the dump had none.

6. **Create each candidate child work item** via `mcp__plane__create_work_item`:
   - `project_id`
   - `name` = candidate title
   - `state` = Backlog
   - `parent` = plan ticket's UUID (this wires the parent-child link)
   - `description_html` = the candidate's description + acceptance criteria (use the same template as `/ticket-new`).
   - Do NOT auto-apply `risk:*` labels — that's `/ticket-investigate`'s call per ticket.
   - Do NOT apply `stub` label. `/plan-new` is explicit commitment; `/brainstorm` is the stub path.

7. **Output summary:**
   ```
   {plan-ticket ID} created: {title}
   
   Guiding principles: {N}
   Child tickets created: {M}
     {child1 ID}  "{title}"
     {child2 ID}  "{title}"
     …
   Open questions: {K}

   Next:
     Review the plan ticket in Plane and prune/adjust children before investigating.
     When ready, run: /ti {child1 ID} {child2 ID} ...     (to investigate in order)
     Or:             /tch {child1 ID} {child2 ID} ...     (investigate + implement with wave detection)
   
   When children ship, run: /plan-verify {plan-ticket ID}
   ```

## Rules

- Separation discipline from step 3 is load-bearing — downstream `/plan-verify` parses the plan ticket's description for principles and judges shipped child PRs against them. Mix the three categories and verification produces nonsense.
- A plan ticket is **optional** — small work should not go through `/plan-new`. If the brain dump describes a single standalone task, tell the user to use `/ticket-new` instead.
- If the brain dump is ambiguous enough that you can't extract 2+ principles and 3+ candidates confidently, STOP and ask the user for more detail. Don't fabricate a plan from thin material.
- Never apply the `stub` label — `/plan-new` is the explicit-commitment path. `/brainstorm` handles the stub case.
- The plan ticket is a Plane work item, not a "new primitive." It's a regular ticket that happens to carry the `plan-ticket` label and have children. Every other `/ticket-*` command treats it the same as any work item.

## Compatibility Notes

- **MCP tool mapping:** In VS Code Copilot, Plane MCP tools are typically exposed as `plane_<tool_name>` (e.g. `plane_create_work_item`), not `mcp__plane__<tool_name>` (Claude Code convention). The Plane MCP pre-flight above detects which prefix is active and uses it for the session. For explicit IDs, prefer direct identifier lookup; if unavailable, use bounded project-scoped lookup + exact match.
- **AskUserQuestion:** Copilot has no equivalent structured-question tool. When the Claude source says "use AskUserQuestion", ask the user in chat with a numbered or bulleted choice and wait for a reply.
- **Parallel subagents → sequential loop:** if the Claude source fans out subagents in parallel (e.g. ticket-investigate multi-ticket, ticket-batch), run them as a sequential loop in one agent session instead. Output and behavior are identical; only wall-clock differs.
- **Backend dispatch:** the "Pre-flight: detect backend" block is authoritative in both hosts. `.claude/plane-config.md` presence routes to the Plane path; otherwise the Markdown path runs.
